Let \(p\) be a prime number. \(p^a\) exactly divides the integer \(n\) if \({p^a}|n\) but \({p^{a+1} \nmid n}\). We represent this as \( p^a ∥ n\).
Assume \(p^a ∥ m\) and \(p^b ∥ n\). What is the maximum power of \(p\) that divides \(m+n\)?
Assume \(p^a ∥ m\) and \(p^b ∥ n\). What is the maximum power of \(p\) that divides \(mn\)?
Assume \(p^a ∥ m\). What is the maximum power of \(p\) that divides \(m^n\)?

If a prime number \(p\) can divide \(n!\) at max \(e\) times, then prove that \(e\) is:

$$\left \lfloor \frac{n}{p} \right \rfloor + \left \lfloor \frac{n}{p^2} \right \rfloor + \left \lfloor \frac{n}{p^3} \right \rfloor \ldots$$
Prove that \(e \le \frac{n}{p-1}\), where \(e\) is the maximum number of times \(p\) can divide \(n\).
How many zeros at the end of 1000!

How many times does 5 divide 1000!? We can use Legendre's formula to find that 5 divides it 249 times. Similarly, we can use Legendre's formula to find that 2 divides it 994 times.

$$ \sum_{i=1}^{\text{some large number}} \lfloor \frac{1000}{5^i} \rfloor = 249 \qquad \sum_{i=1}^{\text{some large number}} \lfloor \frac{1000}{2^i} = 994 \rfloor $$

The prime factorization of 1000! contains \(5^{249}\) and \(2^{994}\):

$$ 1000! = 2^{994} 5^{249} m $$

where \(m\) contains the rest of the prime factors. The integer \(m\) cannot end in a zero because we separated the 2's and 5's so they are not part of \(m\). If \(m\) had a zero at the end, it would have prime factors 2 and 5 which shouldn't be possible.

If we group all the 5's with 2:

$$\begin{align} 1000! &= 2^{745} (2*5)^{249} m \\ &= 2^{745} 10^{249} m \\ &= (2^{745} m) 10^{249} \end{align}$$

The integer \(2^{745} m\) doesn't end in a zero because it is not divisible by 5. If we multiply this by \(10^{249}\), then we get 249 zeros at the end.
